title: Registry never invokes validatePhases — phases.json schema tests don't constrain the registry
description: T-037/T-038/T-039 exercise validatePhases() in isolation, but no test wires phases.json through the registry. The current implementation never reads or validates phases.json — it only validates path.json. A path with a missing or schema-invalid phases.json (e.g. zero-spot phase) is happily surfaced in result.paths with no warning, and no test would catch this. The contract's 'schema-invalid files' clause for the registry is therefore only enforced for path.json. An implementer could ship a registry that completely ignores phases.json (as this one does) and pass green, even though contract.md/Files names phases.ts as a 'runtime validator for phases.json schema-only'.
impact: Phases-schema regressions are undetected at the registry level. A path with malformed phases.json will be advertised to learners and only blow up downstream in cycle 4 when phases are consumed.
recommendation: Add a registry test: construct a path with valid path.json but phases.json missing the phases array (or with a zero-spot phase), and assert (a) the path is omitted from result.paths and (b) result.warnings contains a phases-schema warning identifying the file. Either that, or amend the contract to declare phases.json validation out of scope for cycle 1's registry.

title: T-018 zero-write spies miss fs.createWriteStream and fs.open(write-flag)
description: The AC-1.3 invariant test spies on writeFile/writeFileSync/appendFile/appendFileSync/mkdir/mkdirSync/rename/renameSync/truncate/truncateSync/unlink/unlinkSync (sync, callback, and promises). It does NOT spy on fs.createWriteStream, fs.open/fs.openSync (with 'w'/'a'/'w+'/'a+' flags), fs.writev/writevSync, fs.fdatasync, or fs.copyFile/copyFileSync. An implementer who introduces a debug-trail via createWriteStream or fs.openSync(path,'w') + fs.writeSync(fd,...) would slip past the spies. The recursive snapshot inside tempProjectRoot would still catch writes that land there, but writes routed elsewhere (e.g. to /tmp, ~/.cache, or the cwd) would go undetected.
impact: An implementer determined to log via stream-style writes can violate AC-1.3 silently. Probability is low because the obvious APIs are covered, but the contract calls AC-1.3 'load-bearing' — the test should be airtight.
recommendation: Add spies on fs.createWriteStream, fs.open, fs.openSync, fs.copyFile, fs.copyFileSync, and fsPromises.open/copyFile, asserting zero calls (or filtering to write-flag invocations).

title: T-027 grep scope skips dist/, package.json, agents/, hooks/, root — implementer can hide the slug there
description: T-027 walks only mcp/server/src, commands, skills/course-engine, scripts/e2e. It does NOT walk mcp/server/package.json, mcp/server/dist/, agents/, hooks/, .claude-plugin/, or repo-root .ts/.md/.json files. The contract A10 says 'Engine code (everything outside paths/) contains zero string literals matching 01-orderbook-viewer'. An implementer could embed the slug literal in mcp/server/package.json, mcp/server/dist/index.js (a build artifact present after pnpm build), or .claude-plugin/plugin.json without failing T-027. Note also that the walk silently returns on missing dirs ('try/catch return — vacuous pass'), so deleting an entire scan root would also pass.
impact: A10's parametricity proof is not fully enforced. An implementation could hardcode the slug in build artifacts or manifest files and still pass green, undermining the cycle-1 'zero hardcoded path knowledge' guarantee.
recommendation: Expand SCAN_ROOTS to include mcp/server/package.json, mcp/server/tsconfig.json, .claude-plugin/, agents/, hooks/, and repo-root .ts/.md/.json files. Either (a) walk the entire repo and exclude only paths/, tests/, node_modules/, and .forge/, or (b) explicitly list every directory the contract considers 'engine code'. Also remove the silent-return-on-missing-dir to fail loudly instead.

title: T-029/T-030 source grep is single-file — forbidden patterns can hide in helper modules
description: T-029 and T-030 read only mcp/server/src/outputStyle.ts and grep for CLAUDE_OUTPUT_STYLE / process.env.CLAUDE / process.ppid / parent_process / /proc/ / systemPrompt / system_prompt / getppid. An implementer could move the forbidden probe into a helper file (e.g. mcp/server/src/_outputStyle_internal.ts) and import it from outputStyle.ts. The static guard would pass while the security invariant A13 was violated. T-031 (the runtime spy) covers the read-path positive case but only spies on fs.readFile{Sync} and fsPromises.readFile — it would not catch a child_process.spawn or process.env access.
impact: The static guard for A13 is shallow. A motivated implementer could bypass it by indirection. A13 is flagged in the contract as a 'critical finding' for the security reviewer.
recommendation: Walk every .ts file under mcp/server/src/ (or just outputStyle.ts plus any file imported transitively) and apply the same grep. Alternatively, in T-031 also spy on child_process.spawn{Sync}/exec{Sync} and assert zero calls; spy on Object.getOwnPropertyDescriptor of process.env to detect any CLAUDE_* read.

title: T-002 mcpServers reference check is satisfied by any 'mcp/server' substring anywhere in the entry
description: T-002 stringifies each mcpServers entry and accepts a match if the string contains 'mcp/server/dist/index.js' OR 'mcp/server/src/index.ts' OR the bare substring 'mcp/server'. Any field — including a description, comment-style key, env var, or unrelated path — that incidentally contains 'mcp/server' satisfies the assertion. An entry like `{ description: 'wraps mcp/server build' }` with no actual command/args would pass. The test does not require that the matching string appear in command/args, nor that command point to a runnable file, nor that args is an array.
impact: A1's claim that 'mcpServers entry references the built MCP server entrypoint' is only weakly enforced. A malformed manifest with a server entry that does not actually launch the server could pass green.
recommendation: Tighten T-002 to require entry.command to be a non-empty string and entry.args (if present) to be an array, and to require the command/args[0] string itself to include 'mcp/server/dist/index.js' or 'mcp/server/src/index.ts' (not the entry stringified). Better: actually spawn the command with --version or similar and assert exit 0 in an integration test.

title: T-001 'expect(typeof parsed.commands).toBeDefined()' is a tautology — typeof always returns a string
description: Line 88 reads `expect(typeof parsed.commands).toBeDefined();`. `typeof` always returns one of seven well-known strings, never undefined; this assertion is a no-op. The follow-up `expect(parsed.commands).not.toBeUndefined();` is the real check, so the redundant line is harmless but misleading. Same shape would not slip past the assertions for agents/skills/hooks (those use `not.toBeUndefined()` directly).
impact: Minor — the redundant assertion gives a false sense of coverage but the next line catches the case it was meant to.
recommendation: Replace `expect(typeof parsed.commands).toBeDefined();` with `expect(parsed.commands).toBeDefined();` (or delete it; the next line covers the intent).

title: T-038 phases-zero-spots error matcher accepts mention of phase id alone, not the spots concept
description: T-038 asserts `errMessage.toLowerCase().toMatch(/spot|empty|p1/)`. The phase id 'p1' is in the matcher alternation, so an error like 'Phase p1: id is required' (which doesn't mention spots/empty at all) would pass. The test is named 'rejects phases.json with a phase that has zero spots' but does not actually require the error to identify the empty-spots situation.
impact: An implementer could ship a phases validator whose error messages don't mention the actual failure mode, and the test would still pass. Low impact since the validator returns ok=false correctly.
recommendation: Drop 'p1' from the regex; require the error message to mention 'spot' or 'empty' specifically.

title: T-019 well-formed proof is satisfiable by a hardcoded constant return (modulo T-027)
description: T-019 scans the real `paths/` and asserts the response contains an entry with slug exactly '01-orderbook-viewer', plus title/summary as non-empty strings. A naive implementation that always returned `[{ slug: '01-orderbook-viewer', title: 'x', summary: 'y', ... }]` would pass T-019 in isolation. T-027 (engine-source grep for the slug literal) is what makes T-019 non-tautological — together they enforce parametricity. The pairing works, but the comment in T-019 should call out the dependency on T-027, otherwise removing T-027 in a future cycle would silently break the parametricity proof.
impact: No functional gap today; this is a documentation/maintenance hazard. If T-027 is deleted or weakened later, T-019 alone won't catch a regression to a hardcoded registry.
recommendation: Strengthen T-019 by also asserting that `result.paths.find(...).title` deep-equals the title read from `paths/01-orderbook-viewer/path.json` and same for summary. That couples the test to file content rather than to a fixed prefix, removing the dependency on T-027 for parametricity.

title: T-031 readFile spy list misses createReadStream / open(read-flag), narrowing positive-path proof
description: T-031 spies on fs.readFileSync, fs.readFile, and fsPromises.readFile, then asserts every observed read path resolves to <home>/.claude/settings.json. It does not spy on fs.createReadStream, fs.open{Sync}, fsPromises.open, or fs.readv. An implementer using e.g. `const fd = fs.openSync(somePath, 'r'); fs.readSync(fd, ...)` to read a different file would not be caught. Also, `expect(observedPaths.length).toBeGreaterThan(0)` ensures at least one read happened, but does not enforce exactly one — a probe that read settings.json AND another path via the spied APIs would fail the per-path assertion (good), but a probe that read another path via createReadStream and settings.json via readFileSync would still pass.
impact: The 'only reads from settings.json' invariant is enforced for the common APIs but not exhaustively. Combined with the static grep tests T-029/T-030, the practical attack surface is narrow, but this is a hole.
recommendation: Also spy on fs.createReadStream, fs.openSync, fs.open, and fsPromises.open; assert all observed paths resolve to settings.json (or zero calls).
